---
title: "BASC vs the Standard Synthetic-Control Toolkit"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.2
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 4.6
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    geometry: margin=1in
    colorlinks: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
import os, warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
# MVBBSC (the live B-MV row) draws 4 NUTS chains via NumPyro; give JAX four host
# devices so they run in parallel on a single-CPU renderer (set before any JAX import).
os.environ.setdefault("JAX_PLATFORM_NAME", "cpu")
os.environ.setdefault("XLA_FLAGS", "--xla_force_host_platform_device_count=4")
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from IPython.display import Markdown

def _find_up(name, start, hops=6):
    d = os.path.abspath(start)
    for _ in range(hops):
        if os.path.exists(os.path.join(d, name)):
            return d
        d = os.path.dirname(d)
    return os.path.abspath(start)

ROOT = _find_up("basedata", os.getcwd())
BASE = os.path.join(ROOT, "basedata")
DATA = os.getcwd() if os.path.exists(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "data", "basc_weights.csv")) \
    else os.path.join(ROOT, "review", "basc_westgermany")
DATA = os.path.join(DATA, "data")
```

## The question

Bayesian Adaptive Synthetic Control (BASC; *Bayesian Donor Set Selection in Synthetic Controls*) is presented as a competitive-or-superior synthetic-control estimator. Because the credibility of any synthetic control rests, to some degree, on how well it reproduces the treated unit's pre-intervention outcome trajectory, a minimum bar for such a claim is the pre-treatment fit for the new method compared to those of the standard tools in the SCM literature. This note compares BASC's in-sample (pre-1990) fit on West Germany against `mlsynth`'s own estimators on the identical data (the Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller 2015 `repgermany` panel, original per-capita GDP scale), assembled with `mlsynth`'s `compare_estimators` utility. The `mlsynth` analyses run at render time; BASC is precomputed (its Gibbs sampler runs from the authors' R, `github.com/sll-lee/paper-BASC`).

## Specifications

Standard SCM (s-SCM, per the paper) is run two ways. Outcome-only matches the pre-1990 GDP path. The covariate specification is the canonical Abadie et al. (2015) predictor set:

- GDP per capita, inflation rate, trade openness, industry share — averaged
  **1981–1990**;
- investment rate and schooling — averaged **1980–1985**.

We estimate the fit via two bilevel solvers: `backend="malo"` (the Malo et al. 2024 optimum) and `backend="mscmt"` (MSCMT's nested differential-evolution search). The robust family is `CLUSTERSC` (RPCA-SC and PCR/RSC). As a benchmark outside the SCM family entirely, we also include `FDID`, the forward-selected difference-in-differences estimator of Li (2024).

```{python}
#| label: compare
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: false
from mlsynth import VanillaSC, CLUSTERSC, FDID, MVBBSC, compare_estimators

# --- ADH 2015 West Germany covariate specification ---
COVS = ["gdp_avg", "infrate", "trade", "industry", "schooling", "invest80"]
WINDOWS = {"gdp_avg": (1981, 1990), "infrate": (1981, 1990), "trade": (1981, 1990),
           "industry": (1981, 1990), "schooling": (1980, 1985), "invest80": (1980, 1985)}
FIT_WINDOW = (1960, 1989)          # outcome-matching (pre-treatment) window

raw = pd.read_stata(os.path.join(BASE, "repgermany.dta"))
d = raw[["country", "year", "gdp", "infrate", "trade", "industry", "schooling", "invest80"]].copy()
d["gdp_avg"] = d["gdp"]             # GDP per capita as an averaged predictor
d["treat"] = ((d.country == "West Germany") & (d.year >= 1990)).astype(int)

base = dict(df=d, outcome="gdp", treat="treat", unitid="country", time="year",
            display_graphs=False)
cov = dict(covariates=COVS, covariate_windows=WINDOWS, fit_window=FIT_WINDOW, seed=0)

# One panel, several specifications -> compare_estimators does the rest
cmp = compare_estimators({
    "VanillaSC (outcome-only)":    VanillaSC({**base}),
    "VanillaSC (malo, ADH cov)":   VanillaSC({**base, "backend": "malo",  **cov}),
    "VanillaSC (mscmt, ADH cov)":  VanillaSC({**base, "backend": "mscmt", **cov}),
    "CLUSTERSC (RPCA-SC)":         CLUSTERSC({**base, "method": "rpca", "rpca_method": "PCP"}),
    "CLUSTERSC (PCR/RSC)":         CLUSTERSC({**base, "method": "PCR"}),
    "FDID (Forward DID)":          FDID({**base}),
    # B-MV, live: mlsynth's MVBBSC -- a NumPyro clean-room port of the Martinez &
    # Vives-i-Bastida (2024) model (written from the paper, not bsynth's code), i.e.
    # bsynth's predictor_match=FALSE. Rendered here so its agreement with the offline
    # bsynth row below is reproducible at render, not just asserted.
    "B-MV (MVBBSC, clean-room)":   MVBBSC({**base, "n_warmup": 500, "n_samples": 500,
                                           "n_chains": 4, "seed": 0}),
}, show_bands=False)

# uniform-weight diagnostic (what a naive equal-weighted "SC" would fit)
wide = d.pivot(index="year", columns="country", values="gdp"); pre = wide.index < 1990
order = [c for c in wide.columns if c != "West Germany"]; X = wide[order].to_numpy()
obs_pre = wide["West Germany"].to_numpy()[pre]
uniform_rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((obs_pre - X[pre] @ np.full(len(order), 1/len(order)))**2)))
```


```{python}
#| label: load-basc
basc_cf = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_counterfactual.csv"))        # 2000/2000
basc_cf500 = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_counterfactual_500.csv")) # 500/500
basc_w = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_weights.csv"))
bmv_cf = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "bmv_counterfactual.csv"))          # B-MV via bsynth
```

## In-sample (pre-1990) RMSE by method

```{python}
#| label: tbl-rmse
tbl = (cmp.summary.rename(columns={"pre_rmse": "In-sample RMSE", "att": "ATT"})
       .reset_index().rename(columns={"method": "Method"}))
tbl = tbl[["Method", "In-sample RMSE", "ATT"]]

# BASC in-sample RMSE and post-1990 ATT from each saved posterior counterfactual
# (ATT = mean(observed - synthetic) over 1990+, the same convention as cmp.summary).
def _basc_rmse_att(cf):
    pre, post = cf["year"] < 1990, cf["year"] >= 1990
    rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((cf.loc[pre, "observed"] - cf.loc[pre, "synthetic"]) ** 2)))
    att = float((cf.loc[post, "observed"] - cf.loc[post, "synthetic"]).mean())
    return rmse, att

rmse_2000, att_2000 = _basc_rmse_att(basc_cf)
rmse_500,  att_500  = _basc_rmse_att(basc_cf500)
# 25000/25000: reported from a single long offline chain (seed 200). Its posterior
# counterfactual is not stored or plotted; the exact script and the commit that
# produced these two numbers are in the reproducibility appendix at the end.
rmse_25k, att_25k = 195.516, -389.799
basc_rows = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Method": "BASC (25000 / 25000 MCMC)", "In-sample RMSE": rmse_25k,  "ATT": att_25k},
    {"Method": "BASC (2000 / 2000 MCMC)",   "In-sample RMSE": rmse_2000, "ATT": att_2000},
    {"Method": "BASC (500 / 500 MCMC)",     "In-sample RMSE": rmse_500,  "ATT": att_500},
])
# B-MV: the Martinez & Vives-i-Bastida (2024) Bayesian SCM that BASC extends, computed
# offline with the authors' cited bsynth package (predictor_match=FALSE) on this panel;
# see the reproducibility appendix. Its saved counterfactual drives this row.
rmse_bmv, att_bmv = _basc_rmse_att(bmv_cf)
bmv_row = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Method": "B-MV (bsynth, Bayesian SCM)", "In-sample RMSE": rmse_bmv, "ATT": att_bmv},
])
full = pd.concat([tbl, bmv_row, basc_rows], ignore_index=True).sort_values("In-sample RMSE").reset_index(drop=True)
Markdown(full.round(1).to_markdown(index=False))
```

Quite generally, lower in-sample RMSE is better. Every comparison estimator — including the *robust* `CLUSTERSC` methods, which deliberately denoise rather than chase in-sample fit, attains a materially tighter pre-treatment fit than BASC. BASC's pre-1990 RMSE sits in a ~170–195 band across the three chain lengths (500/500, 2000/2000, 25000/25000) — roughly 3x the plain `VanillaSC` fit (60.8), and about twice the `CLUSTERSC` (88.6 / 98.0) and `FDID` (83.4) fits. Lengthening the MCMC does not close the gap; if anything the best-mixed 25000/25000 chain fits worst, so the shortfall is not a burn-in or short-chain artifact. For reference, *uniform* (1/16) donor weights fit ~21x worse (RMSE ~1289) — a point the Table 7 discussion below returns to.

The most telling row is B-MV, the Bayesian synthetic control of Martinez and Vives-i-Bastida (2024) — the model BASC is built on, before the donor-selection layer is added. Run with the authors' cited `bsynth` package in its standard outcome-only mode (`predictor_match = FALSE`) on this same panel, B-MV fits the pre-1990 path at RMSE 62.2, essentially tying `VanillaSC` (60.8), and returns a post-1990 ATT of about -2075, squarely in the standard-toolkit range. BASC is B-MV plus the donor-selection indicators, and it fits roughly 3x worse (RMSE ~170-195). So the paper's own contribution — Bayesian donor selection — is what degrades the fit; the Bayesian framework it extends fits West Germany as tightly as standard SCM. It also differs from the paper's reported B-MV: the text (p.44) gives a post-1990 ATT of -218, whereas the actual `bsynth` output on the original per-capita scale is about -2075 — nearly ten times larger. And this is not a quirk of one package: a second, independent clean-room implementation of the same method — `mlsynth`'s `MVBBSC`, a NumPyro port of the Martinez and Vives-i-Bastida model written from the paper rather than from `bsynth`'s code, and rendered live as the B-MV (MVBBSC) row above — reproduces the `bsynth` output on this exact panel to Monte-Carlo error (pre-1990 RMSE ~62 for both; post-1990 ATT about -2078 vs -2075), and its 95% credible bands agree with `bsynth`'s year by year to within a few percent. So on the original outcome data the authors say they use, two implementations of B-MV built independently of each other agree with one another and disagree with the paper's table. On these numbers, BASC's own -434 is close to the reported B-MV but far from the one both packages return, and the authors' statement that BASC's counterfactual "remains similar to that obtained from the Bayesian SCM benchmark" holds against the reported figure but not the reproduced one. Reconciling the reported B-MV with what the method actually returns is for the authors to do.

## Observed vs synthetic West Germany

```{python}
#| label: fig-paths
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
cmp.plot(ax=ax)                                   # mlsynth methods + observed
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2.2,
        label="BASC", zorder=5)
ax.axvspan(basc_cf["year"].min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0)
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_title("West Germany: observed vs synthetic counterfactuals")
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The gap of interest is inside the shaded pre-1990 window: the standard estimators track observed West Germany closely, while BASC departs from it *before* treatment (at least, using the RMSE as a guide).

## BASC counterfactual with its 95% credible band

```{python}
#| label: fig-basc-ci
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.axvspan(basc_cf["year"].min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0)
ax.fill_between(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["lo95"], basc_cf["hi95"], color="#d62728", alpha=0.15, label="BASC 95% CI")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2, label="BASC synthetic")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["observed"], color="black", lw=2.4, label="West Germany (observed)")
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.set_title("BASC posterior counterfactual (2000/2000)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## The reported "standard SCM" weights (Table 7)

A separate and self-contained problem sits in the paper's Table 7, which reports each method's West Germany donor weights. The "standard SCM" (s-SCM) column is essentially uniform — roughly 0.04 to 0.05 on every donor, with a single 0.34 spike on Spain — whereas the genuine ADH solution is sparse and led by Austria. Those weights are the entire s-SCM model, so its in-sample fit is just their dot product with the donor GDP series, which any reader can check against the authors' own numbers:

```{python}
#| label: tbl7
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: false
from mlsynth.utils.datautils import dataprep

# Canonical reshape: dataprep returns the treated vector, the donor matrix (aligned
# to donor_names), and the pre/post split -- no hand-pivoting.
prep = dataprep(d, "country", "year", "gdp", "treat")
y7 = np.asarray(prep["y"]).flatten()
D7 = np.asarray(prep["donor_matrix"])
names7 = list(prep["donor_names"])
pre7 = prep["pre_periods"]

# Table 7 "standard SCM" weights (CSDA-D-25-01439R1, p.43)
s_scm = {"USA": .05, "UK": .05, "Austria": .05, "Belgium": .05, "Denmark": .05, "France": .04,
         "Italy": .04, "Netherlands": .04, "Norway": .05, "Switzerland": .04, "Japan": .04,
         "Greece": .03, "Portugal": .04, "Spain": .34, "Australia": .04, "New Zealand": .05}

def dot_fit(wdict):
    syn = D7 @ np.array([wdict[n] for n in names7])         # synthetic = donor_matrix . weights
    rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y7[:pre7] - syn[:pre7]) ** 2)))
    att = float(np.mean(y7[pre7:] - syn[pre7:]))
    return round(rmse, 1), round(att, 1)

rmse_s, att_s = dot_fit(s_scm)
rmse_u, att_u = dot_fit({n: 1 / 16 for n in names7})
Markdown(pd.DataFrame({
    "Weight vector": ["s-SCM (Table 7)", "uniform (1/16)", "VanillaSC (an actual SCM)"],
    "pre-1990 RMSE": [rmse_s, rmse_u, 60.8],
    "post-1990 ATT": [att_s, att_u, -1297.5],
}).to_markdown(index=False))
```

Two things follow, using only the authors' own weights. First, the s-SCM column fits the pre-1990 path at an RMSE near 1,925 — worse than uniform (about 1,289), and roughly 30x an actual synthetic control (about 61). That is impossible for standard SCM: the estimator is by definition the simplex vector that minimizes exactly this pre-treatment error, so its solution can never fit worse than uniform, which is always an available fallback. A weight vector that loses to uniform is therefore not the standard-SCM solution, whatever produced it. Second, those same weights imply a post-1990 ATT near +2,900, with West Germany above its synthetic, while the paper's text (p.44) reports an s-SCM ATT of -159 — a disagreement in sign, not merely magnitude, that no rescaling can reconcile. The Table 7 s-SCM column and the reported s-SCM effect cannot be the same estimator.

The authors motivate the raw scale by noting they "removed the normalization step and rerun the entire west germany analysis using the original per capita GDP scale, consistent with the standard setup of ADH 2010/2015," reporting "the same substantive conclusions." But on that very scale, the s-SCM baseline they benchmark against is not a synthetic control at all.


While the reported results from BASC appear to be roughly consistent with my replication, my concern is that the result of BASC is consistently imprecise compared to its comparison methods, making the advantages of BASC over RSC/RPCA-SC unclear (in the empirical comparisons).

## Bottom line

To be clear: I recognize that in-sample fit is not the sole determining factor in the validity of a novel SCM. For example, in the original Robust SC and Robust PCA SC papers by Amjad and Bayani respectively, the authors remark that their denoising solutions are desirable because they attain similar results as the original method without the need to match on predictors. As we can see here, these results hold even though PCR, clustered PCR/RPCA SC attain slightly worse in-sample fit compared to the baseline model from ADH 2015.

However, it is not my belief that BASC attains comparable in-sample fit compared to the baseline toolkit. This holds even on the original, un-normalized per-capita GDP scale the authors adopt in the revision — the very data on which they report the same substantive conclusions. On that data, BASC has an in-sample RMSE that is ~2–3x that of `VanillaSC` and worse than the robust `CLUSTERSC` estimators — indeed worse than a forward-selected difference-in-differences (`FDID`), which is not even a synthetic control — on top of the fact that it excludes the canonical Austria. The paper claims competitiveness to the baseline toolkit in simulation studies, but the competitiveness on the canonical datasets has not yet been demonstrated.

Taken together, the picture is consistent: BASC does not compete with the standard toolkit on in-sample fit — it does not even match a forward-selected difference-in-differences — and that lack of fit appears to feed through to the estimated effect, whose magnitude (roughly -390 to -700 across chain lengths) is neither stable nor close to the -1300 to -2000 that the standard estimators agree on. BASC is also markedly more expensive to compute (an MCMC sampler against one-shot convex solves). What remains is a method that performs well in theory and in the paper's Monte Carlo, but whose empirical usefulness — at least as presented here — has not been demonstrated.

Separately, and independent of BASC's own performance, two of the paper's reported West Germany baselines do not reproduce with their standard implementations. The Table 7 standard-SCM weights fit the pre-treatment path worse than uniform (shown above), and the Bayesian SCM benchmark (B-MV), run on the stated data and scale, returns a post-1990 ATT of about -2075 rather than the reported -218 — a figure produced independently by the authors' own cited `bsynth` package and by a separate clean-room re-implementation (`mlsynth`'s `MVBBSC`), which agree with each other to Monte-Carlo error. I report these only as discrepancies between the paper as written and what the corresponding tools produce; the reasons, and the reconciliation, are for the authors.

## Reproducibility: the offline benchmarks

The toolkit rows run at render time. The BASC 500/500 and 2000/2000 rows and the
B-MV row are regenerated at render time from saved posterior counterfactuals; the
BASC 25000/25000 row is a single long offline chain reported as a bare pair of
numbers (in-sample RMSE 195.516, post-1990 ATT -389.799). The scripts and data
behind the offline pieces are given below so each can be reproduced against a
known state of this repository.

```{python}
#| label: stamp
#| echo: false
import subprocess
def _git(*a):
    try:
        return subprocess.run(["git", *a], capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=ROOT).stdout.strip() or "unknown"
    except Exception:
        return "unknown"
Markdown(f"Reproduced against `mlsynth` at commit `{_git('rev-parse', '--short', 'HEAD')}` "
         f"({_git('log', '-1', '--format=%ci')}).")
```

The chain uses the authors' own sampler (`safe_chol` ... `run_basc_chain` from
`github.com/sll-lee/paper-BASC`, sourced as `basc_funcs.R`) on the West Germany
outcome and 16-donor matrix exported from `basedata/repgermany.dta`. It is
identical to `scripts/basc_run.R` except for the chain length, and is shown here,
not executed.

```r
# BASC on West Germany, 25000 burn-in + 25000 sampling (single chain, seed 200).
#   -> in-sample (pre-1990) RMSE = 195.516,  post-1990 ATT = -389.799
suppressMessages(library(MASS))
rinvgamma <- function(n, shape, scale) 1 / rgamma(n, shape = shape, rate = scale)
rtmvnorm <- function(n, mean, sigma, upper, ...) {   # q=1, diagonal, upper-truncated
  sdv <- sqrt(diag(as.matrix(sigma))); qd <- length(mean); out <- numeric(qd)
  for (i in seq_len(qd)) {
    p <- pnorm((upper[i] - mean[i]) / sdv[i])
    out[i] <- mean[i] + sdv[i] * qnorm(runif(1, 0, max(p, 1e-12)))
  }
  matrix(out, nrow = 1)
}
rdist <- function(x1, x2 = NULL) {
  x1 <- as.matrix(x1); if (is.null(x2)) x2 <- x1 else x2 <- as.matrix(x2)
  outer(seq_len(nrow(x1)), seq_len(nrow(x2)),
        Vectorize(function(i, j) sqrt(sum((x1[i, ] - x2[j, ])^2))))
}
source("basc_funcs.R")                        # authors' safe_chol .. run_basc_chain

y  <- as.numeric(read.csv("y.csv", header = FALSE)[, 1])    # West Germany GDP (44)
x  <- as.matrix(read.csv("x.csv", header = FALSE))          # 16 donors x 44 years
vt <- seq_len(length(y)); Dt <- as.integer(vt >= 31)        # treatment from 1990
j <- ncol(x); t <- length(vt); N <- 25000; nburn <- 25000   # (paper uses 500000/500000)

chain <- run_basc_chain(seed = 200, y = y, x = x, vt = vt, Dt = Dt, N = N, nburn = nburn, q = 1)

g <- chain$gamma.sample; u <- chain$u.sample
beta <- matrix(0, N, j); for (i in 1:N) beta[i, ] <- (g[i, ] * u[i, ]) / sum(g[i, ] * u[i, ])
y.samp <- matrix(0, N, t)
for (i in 1:N) y.samp[i, ] <- as.numeric(x %*% beta[i, ] + chain$f.sample[i, ])
cf <- colMeans(y.samp); pre <- vt < 31; post <- vt >= 31
cat(sprintf("in-sample RMSE = %.3f   post-1990 ATT = %.3f\n",
            sqrt(mean((y[pre] - cf[pre])^2)), mean(y[post] - cf[post])))
```

The B-MV counterfactual (`data/bmv_counterfactual.csv`) is the Martinez and
Vives-i-Bastida (2024) Bayesian SCM computed with the authors' cited `bsynth`
package (Ignacio Martinez's implementation, `github.com/ignacio82/bsynth`), built
from source and run as

```r
bayesianSynth$new(data = repgermany, time = year, id = country, treated = D,
                  outcome = gdp, ci_width = 0.95, predictor_match = FALSE)$fit(cores = 4)
```

on the `repgermany` panel (`D = 1` for West Germany from 1990) — the same
outcome-only configuration as bsynth's own West Germany example. Its posterior-mean
counterfactual gives pre-1990 RMSE 62.2 and post-1990 ATT -2075.
